map.subtitle: Unit 1: Introduction to Security   •   2 days   •   LO 1.4.A, 1.4.B
map.intro: Everything for this lesson lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–2 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: AI Attack Identifier and Exercise 2: Defend the Target (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — When the Old Tells Stop Working (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 2)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — canonical key in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1 the six AI attack techniques and the Scenario 1D chain, Day 2 the four defenses and solving Scenario 1D, then the quiz).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.
Naming: the CED topic is 'AI-Based Cybersecurity Attacks'; the CED scenario is 'Scenario 1D: AI-Powered Cyberattacks' (the voice-clone phone scam). All folder materials refer to this same lesson.

exercise1.title: AI Attack Identifier
exercise1.intro: Part A is an incident table. Each row describes one AI-augmented attack drawn from LO 1.4.A. Your job is to read the report and classify each incident by the CB-specified technique it uses. Part B extends the same identification skill to reflection items. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives 1.4.A and 1.4.B.
exercise1.narrative: A regional security operations center logged five incidents this week. Every one used an AI-powered tool to augment a familiar attack. For each row, note what the AI tool actually did, what was targeted (a person, a model, or a codebase), and the direction data moved (in to the model, or out of it). You will use those observations in Part A to assign the exact CB technique.

rows: Joined a live video call looking and sounding exactly like the company CFO
Help desk nearly approved a large wire transfer on the 'CFO' authorization
Drafted a phishing email in flawless, native-level Spanish with no awkward phrasing
The old 'bad grammar' tell was absent; the message read as native-written
Crafted clever prompts that pulled a confidential client contract back out of a public LLM
An AI model (its stored inputs/training data)
Sensitive data another user had pasted in was revealed to the attacker
Published the same fabricated 'fact' across dozens of low-quality websites
An AI model (its future training set)
Months later a popular AI assistant confidently repeated the false fact to users
Generated new malware and scanned a massive open-source repo for exploitable flaws
A low-skill actor produced a working attack in far less time than it should take

exercise1.scenarioNote: Incidents are constructed teaching scenarios aligned to the College Board AP Cybersecurity Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2026), LO 1.4.A; the 'Data direction' column is a scaffold for separating prompt extraction (out) from training-data poisoning (in).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which TWO incidents are the easiest to mix up, and what single word in the table tells them apart?

partA.q: Incident I-1: name the CB-specified technique, and explain why 'but it looked and sounded exactly like the CFO' is the very trust the attacker is exploiting.
partA.answer: Incident I-1 is EK 1.4.A.1, a deepfake voice-and-video avatar. The adversary used AI tools that leverage existing voice and image samples to create a digital avatar of the CFO and impersonate them on a live call. Appearance- and voice-based trust is exactly what the technique defeats: the clone reproduces how the CFO looks and sounds, so 'it was clearly them' is precisely the false signal the attack manufactures. The danger grows as organizations adopt voice-based authentication, where a cloned voice can become the literal credential.

partA.q: Incident I-2: name the technique, and explain why the absence of awkward grammar is no longer evidence the message is legitimate.
partA.answer: Incident I-2 is EK 1.4.A.2, native-fluent AI phishing. Adversaries use generative AI tools like LLMs to craft phishing in any target language that reads as though written by a native speaker. For years 'unnatural language' was the tell that separated phishing from legitimate mail; an LLM removes that tell on demand, so fluent, error-free writing now proves nothing about legitimacy. The absence of a tell is not evidence the message is real.

partA.q: Incidents I-3 and I-4 both involve an LLM but move data in opposite directions. Classify EACH by number and state in one phrase per incident, which way the data flows.
partA.answer: Incident I-3 is EK 1.4.A.3, prompt extraction — data flows OUT of the model: crafted prompts pull secure or sensitive information (here, a contract another user had entered, or content from the training set) back out. Incident I-4 is EK 1.4.A.4, training-data poisoning — data flows IN to the model: the adversary publishes false information on websites so it is absorbed into the LLM's training set, causing the model to later repeat the falsehood. Extraction = out; poisoning = in. (The 'Data direction' column is the tell.)

partA.q: Incident I-5: name the technique, and explain in one sentence what makes this use of AI 'especially concerning' per the CED.
partA.answer: Incident I-5 is EK 1.4.A.6, AI-assisted malicious coding. Adversaries use AI-enhanced coding tools to write new malware, modify existing application code for malicious activity, or find vulnerabilities in large code bases. Per the CED, what makes it especially concerning is that it lowers the skill and time an effective attack requires, so a low-skill actor can produce working malware and find exploitable flaws quickly.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: None of the five incidents above is reconnaissance, yet that capability underlies several of them. Describe AI-powered reconnaissance and name one incident it most directly fuels and why.
partB.answer: EK 1.4.A.5, AI-powered reconnaissance, uses AI tools that scan the internet to gather information a target posted on social media and public websites — no account breach required. It most directly fuels I-1 (the deepfake CFO): recon supplies the public voice and image samples and the org-chart knowledge of who to impersonate and who to ask. It could equally feed targeted phishing like I-2. Recon is the profiling phase that makes the later impersonation or phishing convincing.

partB.q: Why is training-data poisoning (I-4) often harder to notice than prompt extraction (I-3), even though both involve an LLM?
partB.answer: Prompt extraction produces a visible event — an attacker receives secret data — and the harm lands on one identifiable victim. Poisoning is diffuse and delayed: the false information sits in public websites for months until it is scraped into a training set, and then the model repeats it confidently to many users who have no reason to doubt a fluent, assured answer. There is no single breach moment, which is why EK 1.4.B.4 (verify against non-AI sources) is the matched defense.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: The exam rewards naming a full attack CHAIN, not just one step. Using Scenario 1D, name the two-link chain by number and explain why naming both earns more credit than naming one.
partB.answer: Scenario 1D chains EK 1.4.A.5 (reconnaissance — scraping social-media videos for voice samples and finding a relative) into EK 1.4.A.1 (the deepfake voice avatar that calls the relative). Naming both shows you understand that recon supplied the raw material the clone needed; naming only the clone misses how the attacker got the voice and the target in the first place. The exam credits identifying the chain because real attacks are sequences, and the defense changes depending on which link you break.

exercise2.title: Defend the Target
exercise2.subtitle: 3 scenarios - Break-the-Link Method
exercise2.intro: Scenario 1D taught one core idea: a good defense against AI works WITHOUT detecting the fake. For each scenario, identify the AI technique in play (its), choose the single CB-specified defense that breaks that link of the attack, and explain why it works even when the victim cannot tell real from fake. The centerpiece is the shared secret word.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): name the technique, name the defense that breaks it, and explain why it works without detecting the fake — for all 3 scenarios.
exercise2.methodHeading: Break-the-Link Method - use for every scenario
method: Before analyzing, write your gut read: who or what is being attacked, and what would you instinctively reach for? Anchoring your own answer first keeps a convincing wrong option from swaying you.
Name the AI technique driving the attack (A.1 deepfake, A.2 phishing, A.3 extraction, A.4 poisoning, A.5 recon, A.6 coding). State what link of the chain it represents.

Choose the one CB defense that most directly breaks that link — shared secret word (B.1), MFA (B.2), withhold data from AI tools (B.3), or verify AI output (B.4) — and explain why it works without the victim ever detecting the fake.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment sub-types: 'family-emergency' voice scam = a cloned voice + manufactured crisis aimed at a relative (defeated by B.1). 'Voice-authentication bypass' = a cloned voice used as a login credential on a system (defeated by B.2). 'Data-leak-by-prompt' = sensitive data pasted into a public tool, later extracted (prevented by B.3). 'Poisoned answer' = an AI tool repeating planted falsehood (caught by B.4). Match the sub-type to the defense.
fields: Why it works without detecting the fake:

scenarios.text: Late at night, a parent gets a call from their child's real phone number. It is unmistakably the child's voice — same accent, same way of saying 'Mom' — crying that they have been arrested and need bail money wired immediately. Nothing about the voice sounds off. (This is the CB Scenario 1D voice-clone scam.)

key.signs: Technique is EK 1.4.A.1, a deepfake voice avatar, set up by EK 1.4.A.5 reconnaissance (public videos scraped for voice samples and to find the relative). The 'real number' and 'perfect voice' are the manufactured trust signals — there is no robotic tell to catch.
key.mechanism: Defense: EK 1.4.B.1, the shared secret word agreed in advance. The parent asks for the family secret word; the clone copies how the child sounds but was never told the word, so it cannot supply it. It works precisely BECAUSE it does not depend on detecting the fake — it asks for something the attacker cannot have. (Verifying through a second channel, the B.4 mindset, also helps before sending money.)
key.subtype: Family-emergency voice scam — defeated by the shared secret word (EK 1.4.B.1).

scenarios.text: A bank lets customers access their accounts by phone using voice-recognition software alone. An adversary clones a customer's voice from public social-media clips and calls in, passing the voiceprint check on the first try to request a transfer.

key.signs: Technique is EK 1.4.A.1, a deepfake voice avatar, with the cloned voice used as a login credential — the heightened-impact case the CED flags as voice-based authentication spreads. Recon (EK 1.4.A.5) supplied the public voice clips.
key.mechanism: Defense: EK 1.4.B.2, multifactor authentication. Even a clone that beats the voiceprint hits a wall at the required second factor (a one-time code or hardware key) that the adversary cannot produce. It works without detection because the system never has to judge whether the voice is real — access requires a second factor the attacker does not hold. Note the secret word (B.1) verifies a person on a call, but here a SYSTEM login is involved, so MFA is the matched defense.

scenarios.text: An employee wants to paste a customer database — names, account numbers, and notes — into a free public AI chatbot to 'find patterns.' A coworker says it is fine because the chatbot deletes each conversation right after replying.

key.signs: The danger technique is EK 1.4.A.3, prompt extraction: some AI tools feed user input back into the model for continuous training, and adversaries can later craft prompts to pull that sensitive data back out. The coworker's 'it deletes conversations' claim is an assumption the CED does not grant.
key.mechanism: Defense: EK 1.4.B.3, do not enter personal or sensitive data into AI-powered tools. Keeping the database out of the chatbot entirely removes the data that prompt extraction would target — there is nothing to leak. It needs no detection because it prevents the exposure up front rather than trying to catch a later extraction. (Asking the bot to 'forget' afterward cannot undo data already absorbed for training.)
key.subtype: Data-leak-by-prompt — prevented by withholding data from AI tools (EK 1.4.B.3).

discussion.title: When the Old Tells Stop Working
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: For years we taught two tells: AI voices sound robotic, and phishing has bad grammar. Both are now obsolete — and 1.4.A.2 exist precisely to say so. That creates a dangerous trap: the better the AI gets, the more confident the wrong instinct ('I'd spot a fake') feels. Across three short windows, your group will confront why detection-based defense fails against AI, debate whether a low-tech secret word can really beat a high-tech clone, and decide which CB defense breaks each link of the Scenario 1D voice scam.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. The bell ringer call came from your grandmother's real number in her real voice. Most people's instinct is 'I would just know if it were fake.' Why is that instinct now a vulnerability rather than a defense? What would it take to give it up?

Question 2. For a decade, 'look for bad grammar' caught a lot of phishing. Now an LLM writes flawless phishing in any language. When a reliable tell disappears, what should replace it — and why can't the answer be 'just look harder'?

items.label: Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share)

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the four-defenses walkthrough
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - Why detection can't be the plan
items.text: A deepfake detector might flag 90% of clones today and 40% next year as the tech improves. A shared secret word works at 100% no matter how good the clone gets. Why does the secret word win an arms race the detector loses? Agree on the single best one-sentence answer your group can write.

items.label: Task B - Which defense is hardest to actually live with?
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: of the four CED defenses — shared secret word (B.1), MFA everywhere (B.2), never feeding personal data to AI tools (B.3), verifying AI output against non-AI sources (B.4) — which is hardest for a normal person to sustain, and what is the realistic cost of skipping it? Record your group's ranking and one reason.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: Break every link of Scenario 1D
items.text: The Scenario 1D attack was a chain: recon scraped the voice, a tool cloned it, and the relative got a perfect-sounding emergency call. The relative cannot detect the fake. Decide as a group: which single CB defense is the DECISIVE one at the moment of the call and why, how a different defense (MFA) would matter only if a system login were involved, and what 'a good defense doesn't depend on detecting the fake' means in one sentence you could teach a younger sibling.

items.label: Our group's verdict (decisive defense + one-sentence justification)
